rule.desc: Using `any` to bypass type checking instead of proper types.
rule.better: . Use `unknown` for truly unknown values, generics for reusable code, or specific union types.
rule.why_bad: Defeats the entire type system. Errors propagate silently through `any`-typed code, discovered only at runtime.

notes.note: - Lazily typing external data (use TY405-VALIDATE-BOUNDARIES) - Third-party lib without types (use @types or declaration file) - Complex generic expressions (simplify or use type aliases)

rule.desc: Using `@ts-ignore` or `@ts-expect-error` to suppress type errors instead of fixing them.
rule.better: Fix the underlying type issue. If suppression is truly needed, use `@ts-expect-error` with an explanation (it fails when the error is fixed, unlike `@ts-ignore`).
rule.why_bad: Hides real type errors. `@ts-ignore` suppresses future errors too, masking regressions.

rule.desc: Using the non-null assertion operator (`!`) instead of proper null checks.
rule.better: . Use optional chaining (`?.`), nullish coalescing (`??`), or explicit guards.
rule.why_bad: The `!` operator lies to the compiler. If the value is actually `null`/`undefined`, it crashes at runtime.

rule.desc: Using `as Type` to force a type without runtime validation.
rule.better: . Parse with Zod/io-ts at boundaries; use type guards for narrowing.
rule.why_bad: Type assertions override the compiler without runtime checks. If the data doesn't match, errors appear far from the assertion.

rule.desc: Using plain objects (`Record<string, T>`) as maps when `Map<K, V>` is more appropriate.
rule.better: Use `Map<K, V>` for dynamic key/value stores. Use objects/interfaces for known shapes.
rule.why_bad: Object keys are always strings, prototype pollution is possible, and `for...in` iterates inherited properties. `Map` has proper size tracking, iteration order, and supports non-string keys.

rule.desc: Functions with inferred `any` return type due to missing annotation or unsafe operations.
rule.better: . Annotate return types on exported functions.
rule.why_bad: `any` return types infect callers, spreading unsafe types silently through the codebase.

rule.desc: Using bare `string` for parameters that accept only specific values.
rule.better: . Use literal unions, branded types, or template literal types.
rule.why_bad: Accepts any string at compile time, deferring validation to runtime.

rule.desc: Using a single interface with optional fields to model variants instead of a discriminated union.
rule.better: . Model variants as separate types with a shared discriminant property.
rule.why_bad: Optional fields make every variant look the same. Callers must check multiple fields to determine the variant, and the compiler cannot enforce completeness.

rule.desc: Using TypeScript `namespace` for code organization instead of ES modules.
rule.better: . Use ES modules and files for code organization.
rule.why_bad: Namespaces are a legacy feature. They don't tree-shake, conflict with ES modules, and are unnecessary in modern TypeScript.

rule.desc: Excessive function overloads when a union parameter or generic would be clearer.
rule.better: Use union types for simple cases. Reserve overloads for when the return type varies based on the input type.
rule.why_bad: Overloads add complexity. The implementation signature must handle all cases anyway, and each overload is an additional surface to maintain.
